title: Three years of mornings
summary: A foreign resident of Saigon writes about what she gradually understood — and did not — about the slow morning coffee, and what it taught her to do when she returned home.
body:
After three years of living in Saigon, I have stopped trying to fit the morning coffee into my own categories. I came from a culture in which coffee is a stimulant, taken quickly, often while walking. For the first eighteen months here, I read the local ritual through that lens and found it inefficient. By month thirty I had begun to suspect that the ritual was not coffee at all, in my sense of the word — it was something else, conducted in the presence of coffee.

The hour is what I noticed first. People come to a curbside coffee stand between six and eight in the morning. They do not order, sit, drink and leave. They order, sit, and stay. The drinking takes perhaps ten minutes. The staying takes between forty minutes and an hour and a half. During the staying, the customer does not check a phone, does not read, does not speak much. He watches the street.

In a busier city, this would read as wasted time. In Saigon it is not wasted, because the street itself is the subject. A woman sells flowers from the back of a bicycle. A man stops a moment to fix his motorbike chain. Three older men at the next table argue, gently, about the price of fish. The coffee drinker does not participate. He registers. The morning is being read, in something like the way a newspaper is read in countries where newspapers still come on paper.

I once tried, foolishly, to bring a book. The owner of the stand brought it back to me twenty minutes later with a smile and the phrase, in English: 'For later.' She did not say it as a correction. She said it as a translation of the local etiquette into a language she suspected I would understand. The morning was for the street; the book was for later.

There are, of course, reasons to be wary of romanticising another country's habits, especially as a foreigner. Many Vietnamese drink coffee fast, on the move, on the way to a long shift. The slow morning is not 'authentic Vietnam' any more than the queue at Starbucks in Hanoi is 'inauthentic.' It is one practice among many, more prevalent in certain neighbourhoods and certain generations.

What I will say, more carefully, is that the practice has changed how I sit in my own kitchen on weekends when I am back home. I make a coffee. I sit by the window. I do not pick up my phone for the first forty minutes. I watch the street, which is quieter and emptier and far less interesting than a curb in District 3, but the watching is now possible because someone, somewhere in Saigon, once handed me back a book.

annotations.meaningVi: nghi thức / lề thói / cách làm cố định
ven lề đường / bên vỉa hè
phép tắc xã giao / quy tắc ứng xử
lãng mạn hoá / tô vẽ lý tưởng (về một văn hoá)

grammarNotes.title: Markers of self-correction: 'foolishly', 'of course', 'more carefully'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Văn xuôi B2 thường dùng các trạng từ và cụm chêm để tự đính chính, hạ giọng tránh áp đặt: 'I once tried, foolishly, to bring a book.' (gắn đánh giá lên hành động trước đây). 'There are, of course, reasons to be wary…' (thừa nhận điều rõ ràng). 'What I will say, more carefully, is that…' (đính chính trước khi đưa khẳng định nhẹ hơn). Các dấu hiệu này giúp người đọc tin rằng tác giả không thiên kiến, không lý tưởng hoá — đặc biệt quan trọng khi viết về văn hoá khác.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B2 prose often uses self-correcting adverbs and parenthetical phrases to lower the volume of a claim: 'I once tried, foolishly, to bring a book.' (judging your own past action). 'There are, of course, reasons to be wary…' (granting the obvious). 'What I will say, more carefully, is that…' (downgrading a claim before stating it). These markers reassure the reader that the writer isn't romanticising — especially important when writing about another culture.

translationVi:
Sau ba năm sống ở Sài Gòn, tôi đã thôi cố nhét cà phê buổi sáng vào các phạm trù của riêng mình. Tôi đến từ một văn hoá coi cà phê là chất kích thích, uống nhanh, thường vừa đi vừa uống. Mười tám tháng đầu ở đây, tôi đọc nghi thức địa phương qua lăng kính đó và thấy nó không hiệu quả. Đến tháng thứ ba mươi, tôi bắt đầu nghi rằng nghi thức ấy không hề là 'cà phê' theo nghĩa của tôi — nó là một thứ khác, diễn ra với sự hiện diện của cà phê.

Giờ giấc là cái tôi nhận thấy đầu tiên. Người ta đến quán cà phê vỉa hè từ sáu đến tám giờ sáng. Họ không gọi, ngồi, uống, rồi đi. Họ gọi, ngồi, và ở lại. Uống mất chừng mười phút. Ở lại kéo dài từ bốn mươi phút đến tiếng rưỡi. Trong khi 'ở lại', khách hàng không kiểm tra điện thoại, không đọc, không nói nhiều. Anh ấy nhìn ra đường.

Ở một thành phố bận rộn hơn, điều này sẽ bị đọc là phí thời gian. Ở Sài Gòn không phí, vì chính con đường là chủ đề. Một chị bán hoa trên xe đạp. Một anh dừng một lúc để sửa xích xe máy. Ba ông già ở bàn bên cãi nhau, nhẹ nhàng, về giá cá. Người uống cà phê không tham gia. Anh ghi nhận. Buổi sáng đang được đọc, đại khái như cách người ta đọc một tờ báo ở những nước báo còn in trên giấy.

Một lần, một cách ngốc nghếch, tôi mang theo một cuốn sách. Bà chủ quán đem trả tôi sau hai mươi phút, kèm một nụ cười và một câu tiếng Anh: 'Để sau.' Bà không nói câu đó như một lời chỉnh. Bà nói nó như một bản dịch của phép tắc địa phương sang một thứ tiếng bà đoán tôi sẽ hiểu. Buổi sáng dành cho con đường; cuốn sách để sau.

Dĩ nhiên, có lý do để cảnh giác với việc lãng mạn hoá thói quen của một xứ khác, đặc biệt khi là người ngoài. Rất nhiều người Việt uống cà phê nhanh, vừa đi vừa uống, trên đường tới một ca làm dài. Buổi sáng chậm không phải 'Việt Nam đích thực' hơn so với cái hàng dài ở Starbucks tại Hà Nội là 'không đích thực'. Nó là một thực hành trong nhiều thực hành — phổ biến hơn ở một số khu phố và một số thế hệ.

Điều tôi sẽ nói, cẩn trọng hơn, là thực hành đó đã thay đổi cách tôi ngồi trong căn bếp của mình vào cuối tuần khi về nước. Tôi pha một ly cà phê. Tôi ngồi cạnh cửa sổ. Tôi không cầm điện thoại trong bốn mươi phút đầu. Tôi nhìn ra phố — yên hơn, trống hơn và kém thú vị hơn nhiều so với một vỉa hè Quận 3 — nhưng cái 'nhìn' giờ có thể được, vì một ai đó, ở đâu đó tại Sài Gòn, đã có lần đưa lại cho tôi một cuốn sách.

questions.prompt: How long has the writer lived in Saigon?
questions.explanation: 'After three years of living in Saigon…'

questions.prompt: How did she initially read the ritual?
options: Through her own culture's lens — coffee as stimulant — and found it inefficient
questions.explanation: 'For the first eighteen months here, I read the local ritual through that lens and found it inefficient.'

questions.prompt: What did she begin to suspect by month thirty?
options: That the ritual was not coffee at all in her sense — it was something else conducted in the presence of coffee
That she had misunderstood the language
questions.explanation: 'The ritual was not coffee at all, in my sense of the word — it was something else, conducted in the presence of coffee.'

questions.prompt: How does she split the 'order' and the 'stay'?
options: Drinking takes about ten minutes; staying takes between forty minutes and an hour and a half
Drinking takes an hour; staying takes ten minutes

questions.prompt: What does the customer do during 'the staying'?
options: He does not check a phone, does not read, does not speak much — he watches the street.
questions.explanation: 'The customer does not check a phone, does not read, does not speak much. He watches the street.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer compare the 'reading' of the morning to?
options: The way a newspaper is read in countries where newspapers still come on paper
questions.explanation: 'In something like the way a newspaper is read in countries where newspapers still come on paper.'

questions.prompt: What happened when she brought a book?
options: The owner returned it to her twenty minutes later with a smile and the phrase 'For later.'
The owner threw the book away.
The owner asked her to leave.

questions.prompt: How does the writer interpret the owner's 'For later'?
options: As a translation of the local etiquette into a language the owner suspected she would understand — not a correction
questions.explanation: 'She said it as a translation of the local etiquette into a language she suspected I would understand.'

questions.prompt: What warning does the writer issue in paragraph 5?
options: There are reasons to be wary of romanticising another country's habits, especially as a foreigner.
Tourists should never sit on the curb.

questions.prompt: How does she characterise the slow morning relative to 'authentic Vietnam'?
options: It is one practice among many — not more 'authentic' than the queue at a Starbucks in Hanoi.
It is the only true Vietnamese habit.
questions.explanation: 'The slow morning is not "authentic Vietnam" any more than the queue at Starbucks in Hanoi is "inauthentic."'

questions.prompt: How has the practice changed her behaviour at home?
options: She makes a coffee, sits by the window, and does not pick up her phone for the first forty minutes.
She stopped drinking coffee at home.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 gives the three small habits.

questions.prompt: How does she describe her own home street compared to a Saigon curb?
options: Quieter, emptier, and far less interesting
questions.explanation: 'Quieter and emptier and far less interesting than a curb in District 3.'

questions.prompt: Which of these is the essay NOT claiming?
options: That the slow morning is 'authentic Vietnam' and the queue at Starbucks is not.
That foreigners should be wary of romanticising another culture.
That the writer changed her own habits after the experience.
That the ritual is in the presence of coffee rather than about coffee in her sense.
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly refuses this binary.
questions.hint: Look for the option the writer takes pains to deny.

questions.prompt: Which sentence captures the writer's reframing of what the morning is?
options: 'It was something else, conducted in the presence of coffee.'
'I came from a culture where coffee is taken quickly.'
questions.hint: Look at the line where the writer admits the wrong category.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the essay?
options: Long exposure to another culture's small habits can quietly reshape a foreigner's attention — without that habit having to be 'authentic' or universal.
Vietnamese coffee is the best in the world.
Books should be left at home.
questions.explanation: The writer takes years to revise her category, refuses to claim it as essential, and notes the practical residue: a different morning at home.
questions.hint: Notice both her concessions and her final small change.

blanks.explanation: Conducted in the presence of coffee.
She tried to bring a book.
Wary of romanticising another country's habits.
Not more inauthentic than its opposite.
How she sits in her own kitchen at home.
She was handed back a book.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer says she eventually realised she had spent eighteen months filing a local practice under the wrong category. Is there a foreign or unfamiliar habit you have judged through your own categories, and which you might be misreading? What would change if you treated it as 'something else conducted in the presence of' the thing you assumed it was?
